metadata.summary: Match cloud instance sizes to actual workload requirements using utilization metrics; CPU/memory rightsizing saves 20-40%, Graviton migration 20-40% additional.

content.title: Cloud Instance Rightsizing
content.summary: Rightsizing matches instance sizes to actual workload requirements. Most teams overprovision to ensure systems do not crash during peak hours, leaving CPU utilization below 30% and memory below 40%. A systematic 4-week analysis followed by phased downsizing consistently delivers 20-40% compute savings with zero performance impact.

why.p: Cloud spending reached $723.4B in 2025, yet organizations waste 32% ($200B+) on unused resources. The single largest source of waste is overprovisioned compute: instances upgraded "just in case" run at 10-15% utilization for months. Rightsizing attacks this directly — the same application runs identically on a smaller instance when actual utilization is well below the ceiling.

Graviton (ARM) and AMD processor families offer an additional 10-40% cost reduction on top of any size reduction, often with latency improvements, because the newer microarchitectures are more efficient per dollar.

list.item: Any cloud environment that has been running for 4+ weeks and has not been rightsized in the past quarter.
Instances upgraded "just in case" for an anticipated traffic spike that never arrived.
B2B SaaS or microservices platforms with 10+ instances where even 10% savings is significant at scale.
Before purchasing Reserved Instances — rightsize first, then commit to correct sizing.
Post-migration: newly migrated workloads are commonly oversized due to lift-and-shift conservatism.
Instances with highly variable or spiky CPU (peak exceeds 70%) — downsizing risks throttling under load.
Latency-sensitive production services where a 5-10% CPU headroom matters — require load testing first.
Fewer than 4 weeks of monitoring data — insufficient to distinguish baseline from anomaly.

section.name: rightsizing-indicators
section.p: Key indicators that an instance is a rightsizing candidate:

list.item: CPU utilization consistently below 30% (4-week average)
Memory utilization consistently below 40% (4-week average)
Network I/O consistently low relative to instance network capacity
Instance type was upgraded "just in case" without a load test justifying the upgrade
CPU/Memory rightsizing: 20-40% reduction on compute line
Graviton/ARM migration (AWS): 20-40% additional savings with performance gains
AMD processor migration: 10-20% savings vs Intel equivalent
Storage rightsizing (GP2 to GP3 EBS): approximately 20% per volume with no downtime

rule.statement: Before rightsizing any production instance, collect at minimum 4 weeks of CPU and memory utilization data, identify peak values (not just averages), and implement the change in non-production first with a 48-hour validation window before promoting to production.
rule.rationale: Averages hide spikes. A 15% average CPU with a 95th-percentile of 85% is NOT a rightsizing candidate. The 4-week window captures weekly patterns including batch jobs, end-of-month processing, and marketing campaigns.

section.name: phased-implementation
section.p: Phased rightsizing reduces risk by validating in lower environments first. The SmartNews case study showed 50% main compute savings and 15% ML savings via Spot + Graviton combined.

A B2B SaaS startup with 50+ microservices found 4-week CloudWatch analysis revealing m5.xlarge running at 12% CPU / 28% memory and m5.2xlarge at 8% CPU / 22% memory — both strong rightsizing candidates.

example.title: Phased rightsizing: 3-month plan
example.code:
Phase 1 - Non-prod environments (Week 1-2): - Downsize all dev instances by 50% - Validate no performance impact over 48 hours - Expected savings: $8,000/month
Phase 2 - Low-risk production (Week 3-4): - Rightsize batch processors and background workers - Implement auto-scaling policies - Expected savings: $12,000/month
Phase 3 - Critical production (Week 5+): - Rightsize carefully with rollback plans documented - Convert to Graviton instances where viable - Expected savings: $15,000/month
Results (real case): $85,000/month → $50,000/month (41% reduction) Average CPU utilization: 11% → 45%
example.note: Always document rollback: monitor CloudWatch for 24 hours post-change with alert thresholds at CPU > 70%, Memory > 80%.

example.title: Rightsizing without baseline data
example.code: # Week 1: Noticed high monthly bill # Week 1: Immediately downsized all m5.2xlarge → m5.large # Week 2: Production outage during end-of-month batch job # Week 2: Emergency rollback, 4 hours downtime
example.note: No utilization data collected. The batch job ran once a month and required the full m5.2xlarge for 6 hours — invisible in a spot check.

section.name: storage-rightsizing
section.p: Storage rightsizing is often overlooked but offers quick wins with zero application downtime:

rule.statement: Migrate all EBS GP2 volumes to GP3 during the next maintenance window; GP3 costs 20% less and provides higher baseline IOPS (3,000 vs 100 for GP2) without requiring size changes.
rule.rationale: GP3 is strictly better than GP2 in both price and baseline performance. The migration requires no instance restart and no application changes.

example.title: GP2 to GP3 migration (AWS CLI)
example.code:
# Find all GP2 volumes aws ec2 describe-volumes \ --filters Name=volume-type,Values=gp2 \ --query 'Volumes[*].{ID:VolumeId,Size:Size}' \ --output table
# Modify a single volume (no downtime) aws ec2 modify-volume \ --volume-id vol-0abc123 \ --volume-type gp3
# Bulk migration script aws ec2 describe-volumes \ --filters Name=volume-type,Values=gp2 \ --query 'Volumes[*].VolumeId' \ --output text | xargs -I{} \ aws ec2 modify-volume --volume-id {} --volume-type gp3
example.note: Savings: approximately 20% per volume. For a 100TB environment this is material.

section.name: database-rightsizing
section.p: Database instance rightsizing requires extra caution because IOPS provisioning affects latency directly:

list.item: Analyze RDS/database instance utilization for CPU, memory, and IOPS separately
Consider Aurora Serverless v2 for variable workloads — scales in 0.5 ACU increments
Review provisioned IOPS vs actual IOPS consumed; over-provisioned IOPS is expensive
Implement read replicas for read-heavy workloads before rightsizing the primary

section.name: quick-wins
section.p: Quick-win rightsizing commands for immediate cost reduction:

example.title: Find old-generation instances (AWS CLI)
example.code:
# Find instances using old generation (m4, c4, r4) aws ec2 describe-instances \ --query 'Reservations[].Instances[?starts_with(InstanceType, `m4`) || starts_with(InstanceType, `c4`) || starts_with(InstanceType, `r4`)].{ID:InstanceId,Type:InstanceType,State:State.Name}' \ --output table
# Savings by migrating to current generation: 10-40% # m4 → m6i: ~20% savings + better performance # c4 → c6i: ~30% savings # r4 → r6i: ~25% savings

checklist.check: Enable CloudWatch/Stackdriver detailed monitoring on all instances
Collect 4+ weeks of CPU and memory utilization data before any changes
Identify instances with average CPU below 30% and memory below 40%
Create rightsizing recommendations report per environment
Implement downsizing in non-prod first and validate for 48 hours
Validate performance metrics after each rightsizing step
Audit EBS volumes and migrate GP2 to GP3 (no downtime)
Identify unattached EBS volumes and delete or snapshot
Review snapshot retention policies and implement lifecycle rules
Analyze RDS instance utilization (CPU, memory, IOPS) separately
Schedule quarterly rightsizing reviews (workloads change over time)

section.name: prompts
section.prompt:
Analyze these instance utilization metrics for rightsizing opportunities:
Instance ID: [ID] Instance Type: [current type] vCPU: [count] Memory: [GB]
Metrics (last 30 days): - Avg CPU: [X]% - Peak CPU: [X]% - Avg Memory: [X]% - Peak Memory: [X]% - Network I/O: [pattern]
Please recommend: 1. Whether this instance should be rightsized (yes/no with confidence) 2. Recommended instance type if yes 3. Estimated monthly savings 4. Risk assessment (low/medium/high) 5. Any architectural changes to consider
